request: We want to notify the right people when a freelancer's mission is about to end.

problem.value: Avoid missions ending without timely action (renewal, extension, replacement, or final invoicing/handover), which risks client dissatisfaction or revenue loss.
problem.evidence: Request implies a proactive need ('notify... about to end') but doesn't state the downstream consequence being avoided.

current_process.evidence: No mention of how mission end dates are tracked today (manual calendar, spreadsheet, or nothing).

success_metrics.evidence: Not stated — presumably 'no mission ends unnoticed' or 'renewal decided before end date'.

actors.value: The 'right people' is named but not defined. Candidates: mission manager, client contact (operational/billing/HR role), the freelancer, admin — likely a combination that varies by case.
actors.evidence: Client says 'notify the right people' without specifying who they are.

business_objects.value: Mission (start/end date), Freelancer, Client, Contact (with role) — all existing platform entities.
business_objects.evidence: Context: Mission is linked to a Freelancer and a Client; Client already has Contacts with roles.

business_rules.value: Lead-time before end date to trigger the alert is undefined, and may vary by client, contract, or mission type.
business_rules.evidence: 'About to end' has no threshold (e.g. 7/15/30 days); context notes rules often vary by client/contract.

workflow.value: Assumed: notification opens a decision point (renew/extend/end mission), possibly with an owner and deadline — not confirmed as pure FYI.
workflow.evidence: 'About to end' implies an actionable moment, not just information.

integrations.value: Channel (email/in-app/SMS) and sync-vs-async trigger (real-time check vs scheduled scan) not specified.
integrations.evidence: Context trap: 'notify' implies who/when/channel, none of which are stated.

permissions.value: Who can see mission end dates, who can act on the notification (extend, replace) not specified.
permissions.evidence: Context: permissions are frequently forgotten until acceptance testing; approval circuits are shared across modules.

config_vs_custom.value: Unknown whether the notification rule (who/when/channel) is uniform or configurable per client/contract, as is typical on this platform.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Platform context: notification routing and business rules are standard client-specific configurability.

constraints.value: No deadline, dependency or regulatory constraint mentioned.

edge_cases.value: Unhandled: mission extended after notification sent (re-trigger?), mission ended early/cancelled, multiple missions for same freelancer/client, no manager assigned, idempotency of repeated alerts.
edge_cases.evidence: Context: end-of-mission is an event that can trigger notifications, final invoicing and validation — but idempotency and incomplete data are recurring traps.

reporting.value: No compliance/audit driver named; likely just needs a simple view of upcoming mission ends.
reporting.evidence: No regulatory or audit language in the request.

acceptance.value: Not defined — e.g. 'a notification is sent X days before end date to Y role via Z channel, and is not duplicated.'

risks.value: Risk of notification fatigue (too many alerts) or missed alerts if recipient list/roles are wrong; risk of duplicate alerts on data changes.
risks.evidence: Not stated, inferred from domain patterns.

questions.q: Who exactly are the 'right people' — the internal mission manager, the client contact (and which role: operational, billing, HR?), the freelancer themselves, or a combination that depends on the client/contract?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty definition) × high impact: this single answer determines the notification routing logic, the permission model, and whether contact-role mapping is needed — the core of the build.

questions.q: How many days/weeks before the mission end date should the notification fire, and does that lead time vary by client, contract, or mission type?
questions.why: uncertainty (no threshold given) × high impact: the trigger condition is undefined and context flags that such rules often vary by client/contract, changing this from a fixed rule to a configurable one.

questions.q: Is this notification a pure FYI, or does it open an action the recipient must take (renew, extend, replace, trigger final invoicing) with a deadline or follow-up if ignored?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred only) × high impact: determines whether this is a simple alert or a stateful workflow with tracking, escalation and downstream automation (e.g. final invoicing).

questions.q: Is the end-of-mission notification and its recipients meant to be configurable per client/contract, or is one fixed rule acceptable for all missions?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) × high impact: on this platform, notification routing is typically client-specific configuration — building it as hardcoded logic vs. a configurable rule engine is a very different scope.

questions.q: What happens if the mission's end date changes after a notification was already sent (extension, early termination, cancellation) — should it re-trigger, be recalled, or be left as-is?
questions.why: uncertainty (unaddressed) × medium-high impact: without idempotency/re-trigger rules, extended or cancelled missions risk duplicate, missing, or misleading alerts.

summary.objective: Proactively alert the appropriate stakeholders before a freelance mission ends, so that renewal, replacement, or closing actions (e.g. final invoicing) happen on time instead of being missed.
summary.scope: Likely: a scheduled check on Mission end dates, a configurable lead-time trigger, resolution of 'who' via role-based contacts (client contact role, mission manager, possibly the freelancer), a notification (channel TBD), and basic tracking that the alert fired. Possibly a lightweight action/workflow attached to the alert (renew/extend/end) rather than a pure FYI.
assumptions: The notification is meant to trigger a decision (renewal/extension/replacement), not just inform.
Recipients likely include a client contact by role plus an internal mission manager, not just one party.
The notification rule (lead time, recipients, channel) is expected to be configurable per client/contract, consistent with platform norms.
Compliance/audit traceability is not a primary driver here (no regulatory language used).
summary.blind_spot: The request names the goal ('notify the right people') but not the trigger threshold, the exact recipients, or whether the alert should recur/escalate if no action is taken — and it doesn't address what happens when the mission's end date itself changes after a notification has already fired, which is where naive implementations produce duplicate or missing alerts.

problem.value: Avoid missed end-of-mission actions (renewal, final invoicing, offboarding, client handover) by alerting stakeholders before the mission ends.
problem.evidence: 'notify the right people when a freelancer's mission is about to end' implies a currently missed/late trigger.

current_process.evidence: No mention of how mission-end dates are tracked today (manual calendar, spreadsheet, or nothing).

success_metrics.evidence: Not stated — no KPI (e.g. zero missed renewals, X-day lead time respected).

actors.value: Likely candidates: mission manager, HR/staffing, client contact (operational or billing role), possibly the freelancer.
actors.evidence: 'the right people' is explicit but undefined — context notes that Client Contacts have distinct roles (billing/HR/operational) that usually drive routing.

business_objects.value: Mission (linked to Freelancer and Client) with a start/end date; end date is the trigger field.
business_objects.evidence: Explicit: 'freelancer's mission... about to end'; context confirms Mission has start/end.

business_rules.value: Undefined: how many days/weeks before end counts as 'about to end', whether it varies by client/contract/mission type, and who exactly qualifies as 'right people' per case.
business_rules.evidence: Core ambiguity of the request; context flags that notification rules and routing frequently vary by client/contract/country.

workflow.value: Undefined: single reminder vs recurring, exact lead time, whether it's a scheduled batch check or an event fired off the mission's end date, and what happens on extension/cancellation.
workflow.evidence: End-of-mission is flagged in context as an event that can trigger notifications, final invoicing and validation — but timing/mechanics aren't specified here.

integrations.value: Channel not specified: email, in-app notification, or both. No mention of downstream system updates (e.g. invoicing trigger).

permissions.value: Undefined: who can configure the lead-time/rule, who can see the notification log, whether external client contacts should receive it directly.
permissions.evidence: Context: permission rules are shared across modules and 'notify' implies specifying who/when/channel — none stated.

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear if this notification rule is standard across all clients or configurable per client/contract (routing, lead time, channel).
config_vs_custom.evidence: Platform is configurable multi-client; notification routing is explicitly listed as client-specific in the context card.

constraints.value: No deadline, budget or regulatory constraint mentioned.

edge_cases.value: Undefined: mission extended/renewed after notification sent, mission cancelled early, freelancer on multiple concurrent missions, duplicate notifications.
edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed; context flags mission end as an event needing idempotency/trigger clarity.

reporting.value: No stated need for audit trail of who was notified/when, but may become relevant if used for compliance tracking of contractor end dates.
reporting.evidence: Not raised in the request; no compliance driver named yet.

risks.value: Risk of notification fatigue or missed alerts if lead time/rule is wrong; risk of duplicate notifications if trigger isn't idempotent.
risks.evidence: Inferred from context's recurring trap around 'create/notify X automatically' needing trigger and idempotency clarity.

questions.q: Who exactly counts as 'the right people' for a given mission — the internal manager, HR/staffing, the client's operational or billing contact, the freelancer, or a combination that depends on the client/contract?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, inferred) x impact(high): this defines the whole routing model; getting it wrong means rebuilding notification logic later.

questions.q: How far in advance should the notification fire (e.g. 30/15/7 days before end), is it a one-time alert or a recurring reminder, and does this lead time vary by client, contract type, or mission length?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) x impact(high): the timing rule is the crux of the feature and directly drives the trigger design.

questions.q: Should this run as a scheduled daily check against mission end-dates, or be fired off a specific event? And what happens if a mission is extended, ended early, or cancelled after the alert was sent?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) x impact(high): scheduled-batch vs event-driven are different builds, and extension/cancellation handling changes the state model.

questions.q: Is this notification rule the same for every client, or should routing/lead-time/channel be configurable per client or contract?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) x impact(high): on this platform, notification routing is typically client-specific — hardcoded vs configurable is a major cost difference.

questions.q: How is mission end tracked today — nothing, a spreadsheet, manual reminders — and is there an existing pain point (missed renewals, late final invoices) driving this request?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) x impact(high): reveals the real problem being solved and whether this ties into other end-of-mission actions (invoicing, offboarding).

summary.objective: Automatically alert the relevant stakeholders ahead of a freelance mission's end date, so renewal, final invoicing, and offboarding actions aren't missed.
summary.scope: Likely: a scheduled or event-driven check on Mission end dates, a configurable lead-time rule, notification routing to manager/HR/client contact (and possibly the freelancer), delivered via email/in-app, with handling for mission extension, cancellation, and duplicate-notification avoidance.
assumptions: The trigger is the Mission's end date, checked either on a schedule or as an event as it approaches.
'The right people' likely includes the mission manager and/or HR, and possibly a client contact by role.
Notification routing and lead-time may need to be configurable per client/contract, consistent with platform norms.
There is currently no automated tracking of upcoming mission ends (manual or absent process).
summary.blind_spot: The request assumes 'notify the right people' is a simple alert, but it hides: (1) who exactly is 'right' per mission — likely varying by client/role, (2) how far in advance and whether it repeats, (3) whether the trigger is a scheduled batch or event-driven, and (4) what happens if the mission is later extended or cancelled after the alert fires — without this, the notification risks firing at the wrong time, to the wrong people, or twice.

problem.value: Avoid missions lapsing without a renewal/extension decision, replacement staffing, or timely final invoicing/offboarding — the notification should trigger human action before the mission's end date, not just log an event.
problem.evidence: Request phrased as 'notify the right people when about to end' implies a proactive, action-oriented alert rather than a passive log.

current_process.evidence: Not stated — unknown whether mission end-dates are tracked manually today or already visible in the platform.

success_metrics.evidence: No explicit success criteria given (e.g. zero missed renewals, no billing gap, no unstaffed period).

actors.value: Likely candidates: mission owner/manager (internal), Client contact (operational or billing role), possibly the Freelancer — but 'the right people' is exactly the undefined part.
actors.evidence: Domain notes: Client contacts carry a role (billing/HR/operational) that usually drives notification routing; request doesn't specify which role(s) or whether internal staff are included.

business_objects.value: Mission (linked to Freelancer and Client, has start/end date); Client Contacts with roles as potential recipients.
business_objects.evidence: Context card: 'Mission ... linked to a Freelancer and a Client, has a start and an end. End-of-mission is an event.'

business_rules.value: Undefined lead time for 'about to end' (e.g. 7/14/30 days before) and whether it varies by client, contract type, or mission duration.
business_rules.evidence: No lead-time or threshold mentioned in the request.

workflow.value: Unclear whether this is a single alert, recurring reminders until action is taken, and what downstream action it should chain to (extension amendment, final invoicing, offboarding checklist).
workflow.evidence: Context: end-of-mission is an event that 'can trigger notifications, final invoicing and validation' — but the request only mentions notification, not what follows or whether it repeats.

integrations.value: Channel (email/in-app/webhook) and role-based routing to the right Client contact not specified.
integrations.evidence: 'Notify' stated without channel or routing logic — a known recurring trap per context card ('notify implies who, when, which channel').

permissions.value: Unclear who can configure the lead time/recipients per client, and whether external Client contacts should see mission-end details at all (data exposure).
permissions.evidence: Not addressed in request; permissions are 'forgotten until acceptance testing' per context card.

config_vs_custom.value: Likely needs to be configurable per client (lead time, recipient roles) rather than hardcoded, given platform is multi-client.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'business rule can vary by client, contract or country'; 'notification routing' is listed as client-specific config.

constraints.evidence: No deadline or regulatory constraint stated.

edge_cases.value: What if the mission is extended/cancelled after the notification fires? What if there's no Client contact with the right role? Multiple missions ending same week for one client?
edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed; inferred from general mission lifecycle edge cases.

reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit driver named for this specific notification; default low impact applies.

acceptance.evidence: Not defined yet — depends on lead time and recipient rules being settled first.

risks.value: Risk of notification fatigue (too many/too early) or missed action (too late/wrong recipient); risk of duplicate notifications if mission end date changes.
risks.evidence: Inferred from context's idempotency and trigger-timing traps.

questions.q: Who exactly should be notified — the internal mission owner/manager, a specific Client contact role (operational vs. billing), the freelancer, or several of these depending on the case?
questions.why: High impact, low completeness: 'the right people' is the crux of the request and directly determines routing logic, permissions and data exposure — building the wrong recipient list means redoing the core feature.

questions.q: How far in advance is 'about to end' — a fixed number of days, or does it vary by client/contract/mission type?
questions.why: High impact, empty: the lead-time rule shapes the trigger logic and whether it needs to be configurable per client, per the platform's recurring 'varies by client/contract' trap.

questions.q: Should this be a one-time alert, or repeat (e.g. weekly) until someone acts — and what action is it meant to trigger (renewal amendment, final invoice, offboarding checklist)?
questions.why: High impact, empty: determines whether this is a simple scheduled email or a stateful reminder loop tied to a follow-up workflow — very different build costs.

questions.q: Should the lead time and recipient roles be configurable per client, or is a single global rule acceptable for now?
questions.why: High impact, empty: on a multi-client platform, hardcoding this now but needing per-client variation later is expensive to retrofit — worth settling upfront.

questions.q: Today, how do you find out a mission is ending — nothing, a spreadsheet, or another report? And what typically goes wrong (missed renewals, staffing gaps)?
questions.why: Medium impact but currently empty: understanding the as-is process reveals the real success metric and confirms the problem behind the request.

summary.objective: Automatically alert the relevant people before a freelance mission ends, so that renewal, replacement staffing, final invoicing or offboarding can happen without a gap or missed deadline.
summary.scope: A scheduled trigger based on the Mission's end date, computing a 'lead time' before end, and routing a notification (channel TBD) to one or more recipients (internal manager and/or Client contact by role, possibly the freelancer). Likely configurable per client given the platform's multi-client nature. May need to chain into a follow-up workflow (extension, final invoice, offboarding) rather than being a standalone alert.
assumptions: The notification is meant to prompt action (renewal/staffing/invoicing decision), not just log an event.
Recipients likely include a Client contact filtered by role (operational or billing), plus an internal mission owner.
The lead-time window and recipient list will need to be configurable per client rather than hardcoded, consistent with platform conventions.
summary.blind_spot: The request says 'notify the right people' but doesn't define who they are, how early 'about to end' means, or whether this is a single alert or a repeating reminder tied to a real follow-up action (renewal, restaffing, final invoicing). Building a notification without settling these first risks a technically-working alert that nobody acts on, or that annoys people with the wrong timing or wrong audience.
